humanities_role_explanation: Uses arts and humanities subjects (e.g., History, Philosophy, World Religions) as test content to evaluate multilingual capabilities and cultural alignment of LLMs.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only multiple choice questions from MMLU translated/adapted into 42 languages.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates models on multi-subject factual knowledge and contextual reasoning, categorized into culturally-sensitive and culturally-agnostic questions.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates LLMs across 57 subjects including STEM, Humanities, and Social Sciences.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 14 open-weights and closed-source generative LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, Gemma 2, Aya Expanse).

llm_is_primary_system.note: LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes humanities subjects like US History, World History, Philosophy, World Religions, and Jurisprudence.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses multi-choice accuracy, Krippendorff's Alpha for annotator agreement, ChrF++ for translation quality, and rank/position shifts.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English and the benchmark includes English as one of its 42 languages.

summary: This paper presents Global-MMLU, a high-quality multilingual adaptation of the MMLU dataset across 42 languages. It introduces annotations that partition the dataset into culturally-sensitive and culturally-agnostic subsets, exposing heavy Western-centric bias in the original MMLU. The authors demonstrate that model rankings fluctuate significantly depending on whether they are evaluated on culturally-sensitive or agnostic data.
key_findings: 28% of MMLU questions are culturally sensitive, with 86.5% of those being Western-centric and 84.9% of regional questions focusing on North America or Europe. Evaluating 14 LLMs on culturally-agnostic (CA) versus culturally-sensitive (CS) subsets reveals substantial volatility in model rankings (averaging 5.7 rank changes and 7.3 position shifts on CS subsets).
